item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
well, this was incredibly chilling. wtf did Schmidt DO? did he intend for it to this awful? doubtful.

anyway. an enthusiastic +1 from me, can't believe i didn't see this sooner. personally I'm not of the opinion that 1 article out of thousands having some similarities to 1-2-3 others, again out of thousands, means it can't be done well and in a way that makes the story all its it's own depending on how it's told. there are bound to be some ideas that get revisited. it's how you do it that makes it sink or swim. for me, this was a swimmer. I was a theater kid when I was really little (lost my passion for it after my dad died when I was 11), and for me this was… well, like I said before, chilling.

I feel sad for those poor kids. they may have left the building but they're still trapped in the play. :-/ bummer. I hope the foundation is treating them well, at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The addendum at the end made me physically ill. Brilliant. +1

item.title: Re: +200
item.content:encoded: Hot damn!
Congrats :^)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Answer:

Upon investigation, it was found that all deceased instances of SCP-2960 had begun normal decomposition. In addition, at 19:00 PST, all living instances of SCP-2960 will enter a fugue state. At 19:30 PST these instances will then act out their roles from the show regardless to context. Use of amnestics has proven ineffective in alleviating this phenomenon.

While the effect keeping the cast and crew within the theater has vanished, it has been replaced by another effect, which means SCP-2960 is far from neutralized. Theater sticks with you, don't you know?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Question: Since the anomalous effect keeping the cast and crew there has subsided, shouldn't this be reclassed as neutralized?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted! I also found it similar but different from the basket game, but it adds to me rather than beign a downer.
Also, someone please put down those poor things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Glad you like it.

On your first point, they could demolish the theater, sure, but as is revealed in the article, this is only temporary as the theater self repairs. Additionally, if they made a hole in the wall, the wall of force that keeps them from exiting by the door would likely keep them from exiting through this new hole as well.

As for updating the containment procedures…
A big part of articles these days is the narrative flow of the piece. The last addendum is what I like to consider the final punch of the piece, in which its revealed that even in escape the damage done to these kids by the good intentions of their theater teacher sticks with them. To play that hand in the containment procedures is to essentially spoil that ending. Granted, I'm sure that in the SCPUniverse, future editions of that document account for such changes, but at the point in time that this version of the document was taken it has not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice and creepy. +1.

Out of curiosity, what would have happened if someone had started taking the building itself apart? I know the instances couldn't walk through a doorway that led out of the auditorium, but if a section of auditorium wall was removed, could they leave through the space where it used to be? If not, would the perimeter still bind them if enough architecture was removed that the space ceased to be an auditorium anymore?

I think the containment procedures need some changes in light of the final addendum. I totally believe that the Foundation would still keep the school locked up even though nothing weird is happening there anymore, but now that the actual people are free to move, containing the site isn't really part of containing the SCP. The procedures should probably shift focus to the conditions under which the individuals are being contained- location, facilities, any restrictions on movement and communication, and so on. (For my two cents, I imagine the Foundation setting them up in a communal facility, where each person has their own private space but can still remain in contact with the others- after five years of total cohabitation, they probably have a strong sense of community among themselves but are starved for privacy at the same time.) The part about psychiatric evaluation and counseling is still good, but quarantining instances who show signs of being suicidal or unwilling to perform shouldn't be necessary anymore, since the show no longer imposes penalties for failure to perform.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Better late than never.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't believe it took me so long to discover this. Tbh probably my favorite part how you completely glossed over the quintuple suicide, leaving the reader to try and figure everything out.

My high school theater director probably would've appreciated this…thankfully most of us weren't good enough actors to warrant a five-year run of anything we put on. Upvoted, despite being several years late.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I can see it, and it hits a lot of similar 'forced to repeat' notes. But I think that there's a major difference between them, which is probably why Season Openers is an SCP that still disturbs me, while this was merely enjoyably freaky. (To be clear, Season Opener scared me in the 'bad' way, in that it gave me a panic attack, especially since my worst fear is time loops, and I was at the game in question, while this was the 'good' way.)

Honestly, I think the thing is they're basically inversions of each-other. Season Opener, you can change what you do, but it's always the same night. Once the reset happens, you can go anywhere you want (only in the arena, perhaps?) In this one, you have no control over what you do, but time advances normally. I feel like both groups would say they'd love to be in the other one's situation. Where the continuity of the actors and the interaction with the SCP agents means that they have some knowledge and community helping them, while the relative freedom in the Garden would appeal to the players. They're similar, but they're two sides of one coin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As an actor, I liked this SCP a lot. The idea was executed very well. Though it is pretty creepy. What causes five people to kill themselves at once? Perhaps being forced to preform the same play over and over was too much. The article does a real good job provoking the reader's curiosity. Were the actors normal kids prior to containment? Is the theater sentient? Is Liam in the theater himself?

It was a interesting article, thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're not the first person to make the comparison between this and "Season Opener" and probably won't be the last. I'd like to argue that both articles are different animals than each other, especially with the Foundation directly able to interact with those imprisoned in this case, but I digress.

Thanks for the feedback.

Shame this didn't do more for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is far too close to SCP-1733 for my tastes, and doesn't really do much with the concept except for add an overly complex process involving death and reanimation. Oh, and Mr. Schmidt as the maybe-villain, which didn't really improve the story at all.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it! The execution on this one was fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Doubt it. I was involved in the production this picture is from, and know all persons depicted there in well. At most they will be mildly amused.

Also, none of them are the skipping kind, so there's that too… :I

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Someone in that show is gonna see that picture in this article.

Most likely, they're gonna flip out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not intentionally, but that's a pretty neat side effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The funny thing is after reading about this Scp, instead of getting scared of theatre and all that I actual wanted to be more in to theatre. Reverse Psychology huh?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Best SCP I've read in awhile. It was actually creepy, and as I read through it, each new section managed to take some new twist or turn which I wasn't expecting. The nature of the anomaly is pretty unique too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heh. It really is a great show to both watch and produce. Glad you got to see a performance of it.

Edit: Out of pure curiosity, what role did they have in the production?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just saw a production of this play. It was really good.

Now I'm imagining the friend of mine who was in the production being trapped in this phenomenon. Welp.

Aaaaand I already upvoted this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have added a picture.

The source can be found: HERE!

The photographer is an old friend of mine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this. I think it's probably partially because I live in Oregon, and there is a school that was closed down in about 2008, and I was there last year, and ooooh gives me the shivers. +1

item.title: Re: nopenopenope
item.content:encoded:
I do aim to please. Glad you enjoyed it so much.

:P

item.title: nopenopenope
item.content:encoded:
I played Hot Blades Harry in Urinetown. I know the show by heart and have very fond memories of it. I can recite every song by heart and say every line.

Conwell, you bastard, have freaked me so much the fuck out.

I had to slam my laptop closed halfway and stop shaking. This is the first thing on this website that has made me do so.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The title gives me chills; that phrase has dragged me into performances I was neither interested in nor prepared for on more than one occasion. But that's the whole point. No matter what, as one director said: "I don't care WHO dies, faints or can't show up. The show WILL go on tonight!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This reminds me slightly of Clive Barker's Sex, Death and Starshine story, except that one had a macabre optimistic undertone and I just loved it.

This one… is competently executed but doesn't really grab me. Like, the letter goes for the "careful what you wish for" angle which doesn't impress me much, and perhaps I'd have wished for something more interesting happening with the ones who commit suicide/die nvm the ones refusing to participate.

Ah well.

Oh, and … serious ibuprofen toxicity happens at about 400mg/kg so the suicide would require about 120-150 tablets.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, well, that's just, like, your opinion, man.

In all seriousness, upon looking over the containment procedures again, implying that the entire school was purchased by a Foundation front organization implies that they have probably taken measures to keep away the general public.

I have removed the above quoted section.

Mmmm'yep.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Former students and faculty are to be dissuaded via a cover story of the building being condemned by the district school board.

1) Dissuaded from entering the school grounds?

2) This doesn't seem a necessary part of the containment procedures. Just put up fences with barbed wire around the school and put up signs about it being condemned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As someone who produced, played the lead in, and directed my high school's senior play, this one freaks me right the fuck out. Good game.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Right, sorry about that. It won't happen again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the article, but please don't add any tags unless you're going to tag the article entirely. Makes the article disappear from the module on my author page, and I don't want to end up forgetting about an article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This really reminds me of Stephen King's work. And anything even remotely like that is truly excellent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It sure is. XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The show must go on.

(Edit: Holy crap, that's actually the mainlist title.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Long winded response to Long-winded 'what if' scenario
– hide block

Actually, you've got it spot on. The barriers would have prevented enough new people (Agents, Researchers, Etc) from leaving the auditorium. Foren would announce a role for them each time there was a show until the original cast and crew was returned.

However, you wouldn't need to whittle away at the cast to get something similar to happen. If all of the Run Crew, or trombone players were removed or busy playing other roles, then the skip would still recruit until it returned to a workable number of personnel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked this in chat Conwell. Glad you posted it! Upvoted wholeheartedly!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really great. It's like the basketball DVD, but in real life, and I don't consider that comparison a bad thing. It's like a living hell for theater students. I think the final addendum really adds a lot to it (it seemed to neutralize itself, but the kids are still stuck).

Long-winded 'what if' scenario ahead
+ Hide block

So, prior to the semi-neutralization, what would have happened if a number of cast members equal to the remaining crew and orchestra members died and their bodies were removed from the play, making the stand-in cast the only ones left, and then someone else died? Would the anomaly have started sucking Foundation personnel in, preventing a certain number from leaving until there were enough to fill all the roles?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno. I didn't care for the supplemental materials at the end of the article, and the accidents befalling the cast members seemed a little too Final Destination-y for me to really take seriously. Just doesn't grab me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't care for the addition at the end. But I upvoted anyways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me of that one high school band that Roget created, but it's unique and creepy enough to work for me. +1.

item.title: Theater sticks with you...
item.content:encoded:
Alright guys, this is the first skip I have posted in a long time, so I hope that you enjoy it.

Before anyone asks, Urinetown is in fact a real musical. One that won two tony awards in 2002. Its actually pretty funny.

A lot of people dragged this one over the coals before it made it here, and I mean a lot. Special thanks to the ones who did the most raking: djkaktus (especially for Liam Schmidt's note), TwistedGears, Jekeled, Athena_Grey and Silber. If I missed anyone else I sincerely apologize.

That being said. Break a leg.
